    ./ci/scripts/trigger-canary.sh --workspace canary-${{ github.sha }}

Observability and artifacts to collect:
- VPC flow logs (1 minute aggregation for canary runs)
- Probe stdout/stderr, tcpdump captures (pcap), and curl/iperf outputs
- NAT/gateway metrics: active connections, bytes_out, error_rate
- Terraform plan JSON and OPA/Rego evaluation result (policy logs)
- Redwood per-tenant egress counters and trace ids for synthetic inference requests

Ownership and escalation:
- Module author: primary contact for Terraform module changes and first responder for policy failures.
- Network oncall: secondary owner for canary network failures and flow log anomalies.
- SRE infra owner: responsible for CI runner, ephemeral workspace cleanup, and automation reliability.

Rollback and remediation playbook (fast-path):
1) If canary fails policy checks: block merge, tag author, and require policy fix.
2) If canary fails runtime probes (egress/latency): disable promotion, destroy canary workspace, and open a P1 with network-oncall.
3) Apply temporary circuit-breaker: attach a deny-egress security group to the tenant or remove route attachments (manual runbook steps documented in runbooks/oncall).
4) If suspected code regression in TF module: revert to last known-good module tag and re-run harness.

Cost and cleanup considerations:
- Canary workspace is ephemeral and should be auto-destroyed within 30 minutes of test completion (success or failure).
- Limit concurrent canaries per account to 4 by default to avoid NAT/Egress billing spikes; increase only with SRE approval.

Operational run cadence and metrics to track:
- Weekly: number of PRs blocked by OPA rules and top 5 policy violations.
- Per-onboarding: canary pass rate and median time to remediation.
- Monthly: upstream false positives in policy rules (policy churn) and probe flakiness rate.

Appendix A: sample minimal terraform output contract (text):
outputs = [
  { name = "vpc_id", value = "vpc-xxxx" },
  { name = "private_subnet_ids", value = ["subnet-1","subnet-2"] },
  { name = "egress_gateway_id", value = "nat-xxx" }
]

Appendix B: quick troubleshooting checklist for network-oncall:
- Verify the canary workspace exists and terraform apply completed without errors.
- Check probe pod health and logs (kubectl logs).
- Pull and inspect pcap from probe; grep for unexpected destinations.
- Cross-reference flow logs for unexpected elastic IPs or transit attachments.
- If NAT connection churn is high, escalate to infra owner to inspect connection tracking and possible port exhaustion.

References and related pages:
- See related Terraform module conventions: /confluence/eng-infra/terraform-and-iac/tf-module-compatibility-and-deprecation-plan
- Canary runner implementation examples: internal repo redwood-infra/canary-runner (link in repo index)
- Policy library: policies/egress in git infra-policy repo

Contact and next steps:
- For implementation questions or to onboard a module into this harness, contact author (Priya Natarajan) or reviewers listed above.
- Roadmap items: integrate Opal for real-time plan gating, add automated remediation playbooks for common policy violations, and surface canary artifacts in the Redwood Console onboarding checklist.

source.title: Private Installer Integration Test Matrix (CI/CD + Build)
source.author: Sahana Iyer
source.body:
h1. Purpose
This page defines the CI integration test matrix for *Redwood Private* upgrades and rollbacks. It is the single source of truth for:
* Which environments we validate (Kubernetes versions, storage backends, VPC vs on-prem).
* Which tests are required for PRs vs merge-to-main vs nightly.
* Release gating requirements for cutting a Private release train build.

This matrix is focused on *upgrade safety*: prechecks, upgrade-plan generation, Helm ordering, backup/restore hooks, and deterministic rollback entry points.

h1. Definitions
* *Private Installer* = redwood-private-installer (operator-facing CLI orchestrating prechecks, plan generation, backups, upgrade execution, rollback).
* *Control plane (CP)* = Redwood Private control services (API/control, auth integrations, upgrade coordinator).
* *Data plane (DP)* = model-serving workers, gateways, runtime components, model cache.
* *N-1 → N* = supported default automated upgrade path (initial GA). Multi-hop upgrades are not fully automated.
* *Online mode* = artifacts fetched from remote registries/repos.
* *Air-gapped mode* = artifacts provided via offline upgrade bundle + local registry/mirror endpoints.

h1. Goals
* Catch upgrade regressions before they reach design partners/customers.
* Validate that installer can *block unsafe upgrades* via preflight checks with actionable remediation.
* Prove rollback can be invoked and completes with post-rollback health validation.
* Maintain a pragmatic CI runtime: PR gating stays fast; deep coverage runs nightly.

h1. Non-goals
* Full combinatorial coverage across every K8s distro and storage system.
* Performance/throughput benchmarking (tracked separately under runtime perf suites).

h1. Test tiers and when they run
h2. Tier 0: Unit + static checks (always)
Runs on every PR for installer/charts/terraform.
* Lint, unit tests
* Plan schema validation
* Helm template render checks
* Basic policy/matrix evaluation tests

h2. Tier 1: PR gating integration tests (required to merge)
*Target runtime:* 20–45 minutes per PR (parallelized).

*What it covers*
1) Fresh install smoke
2) Upgrade plan generation + artifact validation
3) Preflight checks (blocking vs warning)
4) In-place N-1 → N upgrade in a representative environment
5) Rollback command *dry-run* (plan + state machine validation) and one *real rollback* case in nightly only (see Tier 3)

*Merge gate policy*
* Required for changes in:
  * redwood-private-installer
  * redwood-helm-charts (Private charts)
  * redwood (control-plane compatibility / migrations)
* Recommended (non-blocking) for:
  * redwood-terraform (unless upgrade-related variables change)

h2. Tier 2: Merge-to-main integration tests (post-merge confidence)
Triggered on merge to main (or release branches) for relevant repos.
* Broader matrix than PR gating
* Captures issues that only appear with combined changes
* Files issues automatically in ENG + annotates release train

h2. Tier 3: Nightly full matrix + failure injection
Scheduled nightly in UTC.
* Expanded K8s and storage coverage
* Air-gapped verification tests
* Backup + restore hooks exercised
* Real rollback scenario validation
* Failure injection scenarios (DB migration timeout, CP readiness gate failure, artifact mismatch)

h2. Tier 4: Release candidate (RC) gating (must pass to cut a Private release)
Prior to promoting an RC:
* Required subset of Tier 3 must pass (see “Release gating” section)
* Evidence is attached to the release train notes by Release Eng

h1. Supported test infrastructure
We run ephemeral clusters and supporting dependencies in CI.

h2. Kubernetes providers
* Primary: EKS (VPC scenarios)
* Secondary: kOps (for more configurable networking cases) and kind (fast smoke only)
* On-prem simulation: kOps cluster with “restricted egress” and local registries to approximate air-gapped flows

h2. Cluster sizing (default CI)
* CP nodes: 2–3
* DP nodes: 2 (GPU not required for upgrade tests; DP charts validated for readiness and rollout ordering)
* DB: Postgres (managed-like for VPC; self-hosted for on-prem simulation)

h2. Artifact sources
* Online mode: Redwood staging registries + chart repo
* Air-gapped mode: offline bundle stored in CI artifact store + local registry mirror container

h1. Kubernetes version policy
We test a rolling window: *current supported* + *next* + *lowest supported*. When a K8s version is added/removed from support, we update this page and the pipeline config in the same PR.

*Current CI coverage targets*
|| Category || Versions || Notes ||
| Lowest supported | 1.27 | Included in nightly and RC gating | 
| Current default | 1.28 | Primary PR gating target | 
| Newer supported | 1.29 | Included in nightly; add PR gating when adoption increases | 
| Next (preview) | 1.30 | Nightly “canary”; failures are tracked but do not block releases unless elevated | 

h1. Storage backend matrix
We cover the storage systems most likely to impact backup/snapshot hooks and Helm upgrade safety.

|| Storage backend || Where tested || Why it matters ||
| EBS (gp3) via CSI | PR gating + nightly | Snapshot hooks + PV behavior | 
| EFS | Nightly | RWX semantics; common in CP shared assets | 
| CSI hostPath (kind) | Smoke only | Fast feedback; not representative | 
| Ceph/Rook (CSI) | Nightly (best-effort) | On-prem common; snapshot/restore differences | 
| Longhorn | Nightly (best-effort) | On-prem common; PV attachment edge cases | 

*Notes*
* “Best-effort” means we run it nightly and triage failures as P1 if repeatable; we do not block PR merges on single-run flakes.

h1. Environment scenarios (VPC vs on-prem)
We classify test scenarios by constraints that affect upgrade reliability.

|| Scenario || Connectivity || Artifact flow || Primary risks || Coverage tier ||
| VPC (standard) | Full egress | Online registries/repos | Version gating; Helm ordering; migrations | Tier 1/2/3 | 
| VPC (restricted egress) | Limited egress | Mirror endpoints | Artifact reachability, TLS/DNS | Tier 3 + RC | 
| On-prem (connected) | Egress allowed but controlled | Online or mirror | DNS/TLS, RBAC/SSO, storage variability | Tier 3 | 
| On-prem (air-gapped) | No egress | Offline bundle + local registry | Bundle verification, missing artifacts, rollback safety | Tier 3 + RC | 

h1. Required test cases (what each pipeline must validate)
h2. Common assertions (all tiers)
* Installer outputs an *upgrade plan artifact* and persists it in the run workspace.
* Preflight checks produce structured results with:
  * severity (block/warn/info)
  * remediation pointers
  * stable check IDs for dashboards
* Helm upgrade respects CP/DP ordering and readiness gates.
* Post-upgrade health verification passes:
  * CP deployments ready
  * DP deployments rolled out (or explicitly skipped if configured)
  * DB migrations in expected state (no pending locks; schema version recorded)

h2. Test case catalog
|| ID || Test case || Pass criteria || Tiers ||
| IT-01 | Fresh install smoke | All core namespaces ready; API health endpoint OK | 1/2/3/RC | 
| IT-02 | Upgrade plan generation | Plan includes staged CP then DP steps; includes rollback entry points; artifact URLs resolved | 1/2/3/RC | 
| IT-03 | Preflight: cluster health | Blocks if nodes not Ready; blocks on critical PodDisruptionBudget violations | 1/2/3/RC | 
| IT-04 | Preflight: storage headroom | Blocks when below threshold; warning when near threshold | 1/2/3/RC | 
| IT-05 | Preflight: DB connectivity | Blocks if cannot reach DB or auth fails; emits remediation | 1/2/3/RC | 
| IT-06 | Preflight: RBAC/SSO reachability | Warns or blocks based on configuration; must be deterministic | 1/2/3/RC | 
| IT-07 | N-1 → N upgrade | Upgrade completes; CP before DP; audit events emitted; no crash loops | 1/2/3/RC | 
| IT-08 | Backup hook (snapshot/logical) | Backup completes; verification step confirms restore point exists | 3/RC | 
| IT-09 | Rollback (real) after induced failure | Rollback returns system to N-1; post-rollback health passes | 3/RC | 
| IT-10 | Air-gapped bundle verification | Bundle checksum/signature verified; missing artifact fails fast with clear error | 3/RC | 

h1. CI matrix (what runs where)
The table below is the *authoritative matrix* for gating.

h2. Tier 1 (PR gating) matrix
|| Dimension || Default || Variants ||
| K8s version | 1.28 | none | 
| Storage | EBS gp3 | none | 
| Scenario | VPC (standard) | none | 
| Artifact flow | Online | none | 
| Upgrade path | N-1 → N | none | 
| Tests | IT-01..IT-07 | rollback is dry-run only | 

h2. Tier 2 (merge-to-main) matrix
|| Dimension || Values ||
| K8s version | 1.28, 1.29 | 
| Storage | EBS gp3 | 
| Scenario | VPC (standard) | 
| Artifact flow | Online | 
| Tests | IT-01..IT-07 | 

h2. Tier 3 (nightly) matrix
|| Dimension || Values ||
| K8s version | 1.27, 1.28, 1.29, 1.30 (preview) | 
| Storage | EBS gp3, EFS, Ceph/Rook (best-effort), Longhorn (best-effort) | 
| Scenario | VPC (standard), VPC (restricted egress), on-prem (connected sim), on-prem (air-gapped sim) | 
| Artifact flow | Online, Mirror endpoints, Offline bundle | 
| Tests | IT-01..IT-10 (includes backup + real rollback) | 

h2. RC gating (minimum required before release promotion)
*Must pass (hard gate)*
* K8s 1.27 + EBS gp3 + VPC (standard) + Online: IT-01..IT-10
* K8s 1.28 + EBS gp3 + VPC (restricted egress) + Mirror: IT-01..IT-07 + IT-10
* K8s 1.28 + EBS gp3 + On-prem (air-gapped sim) + Offline bundle: IT-02 + IT-07 + IT-10 + IT-09

*Soft signals (review required if failing)*
* K8s 1.29 coverage
* EFS coverage
* Ceph/Rook and Longhorn (best-effort)

h1. Job orchestration and naming
h2. GitHub Actions / CI job families
(Exact implementation may vary per repo; names are standardized for dashboards.)
* *private-it-pr-gate* (Tier 1)
* *private-it-post-merge* (Tier 2)
* *private-it-nightly-matrix* (Tier 3)
* *private-it-rc-gate* (Tier 4)

h2. Artifact retention
* Tier 1/2: retain logs + plan artifacts for 14 days
* Tier 3: retain for 30 days
* RC gating: retain for 180 days (audit evidence for enterprise customers)

h2. What each job must upload
* Installer logs (structured + human-readable)
* Upgrade plan artifact (JSON)
* Precheck result bundle
* Helm release state snapshots (selected namespaces)
* Backup/restore verification logs (Tier 3/RC)
* Rollback run logs (Tier 3/RC)

h1. Failure injection (nightly + RC)
We intentionally induce failures to validate rollback and determinism.

|| Failure mode || How induced || Expected behavior ||
| DB migration lock timeout | Reduce lock timeout; create competing lock | Upgrade halts; operator-friendly error; rollback succeeds | 
| Control-plane readiness gate failure | Block CP readiness endpoint / force crashloop | DP rollout must not start; rollback path remains valid | 
| Artifact mismatch | Swap bundle manifest checksum / missing chart | Installer fails fast during prechecks/verification | 
| Insufficient storage headroom | Fill PV to cross threshold | Precheck blocks with remediation guidance | 

h1. Flakiness policy
* Tier 1 flake tolerance: *zero*. A flaky Tier 1 test is treated as a release blocker until fixed or removed.
* Tier 3 best-effort jobs may retry once automatically. If the same signature fails 2 nights in a row, file an issue and mark as *P1 if upgrade/rollback correctness is affected*.
* Any failure in IT-07/IT-09/IT-10 in RC gating is a hard stop.

h1. Ownership and escalation
* DRI (CI matrix + pipelines): *Sahana Iyer* (CI/integration test owner)
* Release gating approvals: *Connor O’Brien* (Release Engineering)
* Infra dependencies (clusters/storage/mirrors): *Irene Choi* (Infra)
* Rollback correctness triage: *Rafael Mendes* (On-call & reliability)
* Nightly failure triage backup: *Leila Farouk* (QA automation)

Escalation path for a blocking failure:
1) Open incident-style triage thread in #eng-releases and #eng-sre
2) Assign DRI + repo owners; determine if revert is required
3) If within an upgrade window or RC: halt promotion until green or exception approved by SRE + Release Eng

h1. Change process for this matrix
Any change to:
* supported K8s versions
* required RC gating subset
* storage backend coverage
must be made via PR to the pipeline configuration *and* an update to this Confluence page in the same change.

h1. Appendix: Thresholds used in precheck-related tests
These values are test defaults; production thresholds may be configured per customer.

|| Check || Default behavior in tests ||
| Storage headroom | Block if < 15% free on relevant PVs; warn if 15–25% | 
| Node readiness | Block if any CP node NotReady; warn if DP nodes NotReady and DP upgrade is skipped | 
| DB connectivity | Block if cannot connect within 10s or auth fails | 
| Artifact availability | Block if required charts/images not resolvable (online/mirror) or bundle verification fails (air-gapped) | 

h1. Appendix: Known gaps (tracked)
* Deterministic Ceph/Rook environment in CI is still best-effort (work ongoing to reduce flakes).
* GPU-specific DP behaviors are not validated in this suite; this suite validates rollout ordering/readiness gates only.
* Multi-hop upgrades (N-2 → N) are not covered; customers must follow N-1 → N per compatibility policy.

* Nightly failure triage backup: *Leila Farouk* (QA automation)

Escalation path for a blocking failure:
1) Open incident-style triage thread in #eng-releases and #eng-sre
2) Assign DRI + repo owners; determine if revert is required
3) If within an upgrade window or RC: halt promotion until green or exception approved by SRE + Release Eng

h1. Change process for this matrix
Any change to:
* supported K8s versions
* required RC gating subset
* storage backend coverage
must be made via PR to the pipeline configuration *and* an update to this Confluence page in the same change.

h1. Appendix: Thresholds used in precheck-related tests
These values are test defaults; production thresholds may be configured per customer.

|| Check || Default behavior in tests ||
| Storage headroom | Block if < 15% free on relevant PVs; warn if 15–25% | 
| Node readiness | Block if any CP node NotReady; warn if DP nodes NotReady and DP upgrade is skipped | 
| DB connectivity | Block if cannot connect within 10s or auth fails | 
| Artifact availability | Block if required charts/images not resolvable (online/mirror) or bundle verification fails (air-gapped) | 

h1. Appendix: Known gaps (tracked)
* Deterministic Ceph/Rook environment in CI is still best-effort (work ongoing to reduce flakes).
* GPU-specific DP behaviors are not validated in this suite; this suite validates rollout ordering/readiness gates only.
* Multi-hop upgrades (N-2 → N) are not covered; customers must follow N-1 → N per compatibility policy.
text:
h1. Purpose
This page defines the CI integration test matrix for *Redwood Private* upgrades and rollbacks. It is the single source of truth for:
* Which environments we validate (Kubernetes versions, storage backends, VPC vs on-prem).
* Which tests are required for PRs vs merge-to-main vs nightly.
* Release gating requirements for cutting a Private release train build.

This matrix is focused on *upgrade safety*: prechecks, upgrade-plan generation, Helm ordering, backup/restore hooks, and deterministic rollback entry points.

h1. Definitions
* *Private Installer* = redwood-private-installer (operator-facing CLI orchestrating prechecks, plan generation, backups, upgrade execution, rollback).
* *Control plane (CP)* = Redwood Private control services (API/control, auth integrations, upgrade coordinator).
* *Data plane (DP)* = model-serving workers, gateways, runtime components, model cache.
* *N-1 → N* = supported default automated upgrade path (initial GA). Multi-hop upgrades are not fully automated.
* *Online mode* = artifacts fetched from remote registries/repos.
* *Air-gapped mode* = artifacts provided via offline upgrade bundle + local registry/mirror endpoints.

h1. Goals
* Catch upgrade regressions before they reach design partners/customers.
* Validate that installer can *block unsafe upgrades* via preflight checks with actionable remediation.
* Prove rollback can be invoked and completes with post-rollback health validation.
* Maintain a pragmatic CI runtime: PR gating stays fast; deep coverage runs nightly.

h1. Non-goals
* Full combinatorial coverage across every K8s distro and storage system.
* Performance/throughput benchmarking (tracked separately under runtime perf suites).

h1. Test tiers and when they run
h2. Tier 0: Unit + static checks (always)
Runs on every PR for installer/charts/terraform.
* Lint, unit tests
* Plan schema validation
* Helm template render checks
* Basic policy/matrix evaluation tests

h2. Tier 1: PR gating integration tests (required to merge)
*Target runtime:* 20–45 minutes per PR (parallelized).

*What it covers*
1) Fresh install smoke
2) Upgrade plan generation + artifact validation
3) Preflight checks (blocking vs warning)
4) In-place N-1 → N upgrade in a representative environment
5) Rollback command *dry-run* (plan + state machine validation) and one *real rollback* case in nightly only (see Tier 3)

*Merge gate policy*
* Required for changes in:
  * redwood-private-installer
  * redwood-helm-charts (Private charts)
  * redwood (control-plane compatibility / migrations)
* Recommended (non-blocking) for:
  * redwood-terraform (unless upgrade-related variables change)

h2. Tier 2: Merge-to-main integration tests (post-merge confidence)
Triggered on merge to main (or release branches) for relevant repos.
* Broader matrix than PR gating
* Captures issues that only appear with combined changes
* Files issues automatically in ENG + annotates release train

h2. Tier 3: Nightly full matrix + failure injection
Scheduled nightly in UTC.
* Expanded K8s and storage coverage
* Air-gapped verification tests
* Backup + restore hooks exercised
* Real rollback scenario validation
* Failure injection scenarios (DB migration timeout, CP readiness gate failure, artifact mismatch)

h2. Tier 4: Release candidate (RC) gating (must pass to cut a Private release)
Prior to promoting an RC:
* Required subset of Tier 3 must pass (see “Release gating” section)
* Evidence is attached to the release train notes by Release Eng

h1. Supported test infrastructure
We run ephemeral clusters and supporting dependencies in CI.

h2. Kubernetes providers
* Primary: EKS (VPC scenarios)
* Secondary: kOps (for more configurable networking cases) and kind (fast smoke only)
* On-prem simulation: kOps cluster with “restricted egress” and local registries to approximate air-gapped flows

h2. Cluster sizing (default CI)
* CP nodes: 2–3
* DP nodes: 2 (GPU not required for upgrade tests; DP charts validated for readiness and rollout ordering)
* DB: Postgres (managed-like for VPC; self-hosted for on-prem simulation)

h2. Artifact sources
* Online mode: Redwood staging registries + chart repo
* Air-gapped mode: offline bundle stored in CI artifact store + local registry mirror container

h1. Kubernetes version policy
We test a rolling window: *current supported* + *next* + *lowest supported*. When a K8s version is added/removed from support, we update this page and the pipeline config in the same PR.

*Current CI coverage targets*
|| Category || Versions || Notes ||
| Lowest supported | 1.27 | Included in nightly and RC gating | 
| Current default | 1.28 | Primary PR gating target | 
| Newer supported | 1.29 | Included in nightly; add PR gating when adoption increases | 
| Next (preview) | 1.30 | Nightly “canary”; failures are tracked but do not block releases unless elevated | 

h1. Storage backend matrix
We cover the storage systems most likely to impact backup/snapshot hooks and Helm upgrade safety.

|| Storage backend || Where tested || Why it matters ||
| EBS (gp3) via CSI | PR gating + nightly | Snapshot hooks + PV behavior | 
| EFS | Nightly | RWX semantics; common in CP shared assets | 
| CSI hostPath (kind) | Smoke only | Fast feedback; not representative | 
| Ceph/Rook (CSI) | Nightly (best-effort) | On-prem common; snapshot/restore differences | 
| Longhorn | Nightly (best-effort) | On-prem common; PV attachment edge cases | 

*Notes*
* “Best-effort” means we run it nightly and triage failures as P1 if repeatable; we do not block PR merges on single-run flakes.

h1. Environment scenarios (VPC vs on-prem)
We classify test scenarios by constraints that affect upgrade reliability.

|| Scenario || Connectivity || Artifact flow || Primary risks || Coverage tier ||
| VPC (standard) | Full egress | Online registries/repos | Version gating; Helm ordering; migrations | Tier 1/2/3 | 
| VPC (restricted egress) | Limited egress | Mirror endpoints | Artifact reachability, TLS/DNS | Tier 3 + RC | 
| On-prem (connected) | Egress allowed but controlled | Online or mirror | DNS/TLS, RBAC/SSO, storage variability | Tier 3 | 
| On-prem (air-gapped) | No egress | Offline bundle + local registry | Bundle verification, missing artifacts, rollback safety | Tier 3 + RC | 

h1. Required test cases (what each pipeline must validate)
h2. Common assertions (all tiers)
* Installer outputs an *upgrade plan artifact* and persists it in the run workspace.
* Preflight checks produce structured results with:
  * severity (block/warn/info)
  * remediation pointers
  * stable check IDs for dashboards
* Helm upgrade respects CP/DP ordering and readiness gates.
* Post-upgrade health verification passes:
  * CP deployments ready
  * DP deployments rolled out (or explicitly skipped if configured)
  * DB migrations in expected state (no pending locks; schema version recorded)

h2. Test case catalog
|| ID || Test case || Pass criteria || Tiers ||
| IT-01 | Fresh install smoke | All core namespaces ready; API health endpoint OK | 1/2/3/RC | 
| IT-02 | Upgrade plan generation | Plan includes staged CP then DP steps; includes rollback entry points; artifact URLs resolved | 1/2/3/RC | 
| IT-03 | Preflight: cluster health | Blocks if nodes not Ready; blocks on critical PodDisruptionBudget violations | 1/2/3/RC | 
| IT-04 | Preflight: storage headroom | Blocks when below threshold; warning when near threshold | 1/2/3/RC | 
| IT-05 | Preflight: DB connectivity | Blocks if cannot reach DB or auth fails; emits remediation | 1/2/3/RC | 
| IT-06 | Preflight: RBAC/SSO reachability | Warns or blocks based on configuration; must be deterministic | 1/2/3/RC | 
| IT-07 | N-1 → N upgrade | Upgrade completes; CP before DP; audit events emitted; no crash loops | 1/2/3/RC | 
| IT-08 | Backup hook (snapshot/logical) | Backup completes; verification step confirms restore point exists | 3/RC | 
| IT-09 | Rollback (real) after induced failure | Rollback returns system to N-1; post-rollback health passes | 3/RC | 
| IT-10 | Air-gapped bundle verification | Bundle checksum/signature verified; missing artifact fails fast with clear error | 3/RC | 

h1. CI matrix (what runs where)
The table below is the *authoritative matrix* for gating.

h2. Tier 1 (PR gating) matrix
|| Dimension || Default || Variants ||
| K8s version | 1.28 | none | 
| Storage | EBS gp3 | none | 
| Scenario | VPC (standard) | none | 
| Artifact flow | Online | none | 
| Upgrade path | N-1 → N | none | 
| Tests | IT-01..IT-07 | rollback is dry-run only | 

h2. Tier 2 (merge-to-main) matrix
|| Dimension || Values ||
| K8s version | 1.28, 1.29 | 
| Storage | EBS gp3 | 
| Scenario | VPC (standard) | 
| Artifact flow | Online | 
| Tests | IT-01..IT-07 | 

h2. Tier 3 (nightly) matrix
|| Dimension || Values ||
| K8s version | 1.27, 1.28, 1.29, 1.30 (preview) | 
| Storage | EBS gp3, EFS, Ceph/Rook (best-effort), Longhorn (best-effort) | 
| Scenario | VPC (standard), VPC (restricted egress), on-prem (connected sim), on-prem (air-gapped sim) | 
| Artifact flow | Online, Mirror endpoints, Offline bundle | 
| Tests | IT-01..IT-10 (includes backup + real rollback) | 

h2. RC gating (minimum required before release promotion)
*Must pass (hard gate)*
* K8s 1.27 + EBS gp3 + VPC (standard) + Online: IT-01..IT-10
* K8s 1.28 + EBS gp3 + VPC (restricted egress) + Mirror: IT-01..IT-07 + IT-10
* K8s 1.28 + EBS gp3 + On-prem (air-gapped sim) + Offline bundle: IT-02 + IT-07 + IT-10 + IT-09

*Soft signals (review required if failing)*
* K8s 1.29 coverage
* EFS coverage
* Ceph/Rook and Longhorn (best-effort)

h1. Job orchestration and naming
h2. GitHub Actions / CI job families
(Exact implementation may vary per repo; names are standardized for dashboards.)
* *private-it-pr-gate* (Tier 1)
* *private-it-post-merge* (Tier 2)
* *private-it-nightly-matrix* (Tier 3)
* *private-it-rc-gate* (Tier 4)

h2. Artifact retention
* Tier 1/2: retain logs + plan artifacts for 14 days
* Tier 3: retain for 30 days
* RC gating: retain for 180 days (audit evidence for enterprise customers)

h2. What each job must upload
* Installer logs (structured + human-readable)
* Upgrade plan artifact (JSON)
* Precheck result bundle
* Helm release state snapshots (selected namespaces)
* Backup/restore verification logs (Tier 3/RC)
* Rollback run logs (Tier 3/RC)

h1. Failure injection (nightly + RC)
We intentionally induce failures to validate rollback and determinism.

|| Failure mode || How induced || Expected behavior ||
| DB migration lock timeout | Reduce lock timeout; create competing lock | Upgrade halts; operator-friendly error; rollback succeeds | 
| Control-plane readiness gate failure | Block CP readiness endpoint / force crashloop | DP rollout must not start; rollback path remains valid | 
| Artifact mismatch | Swap bundle manifest checksum / missing chart | Installer fails fast during prechecks/verification | 
| Insufficient storage headroom | Fill PV to cross threshold | Precheck blocks with remediation guidance | 

h1. Flakiness policy
* Tier 1 flake tolerance: *zero*. A flaky Tier 1 test is treated as a release blocker until fixed or removed.
* Tier 3 best-effort jobs may retry once automatically. If the same signature fails 2 nights in a row, file an issue and mark as *P1 if upgrade/rollback correctness is affected*.
* Any failure in IT-07/IT-09/IT-10 in RC gating is a hard stop.

h1. Ownership and escalation
* DRI (CI matrix + pipelines): *Sahana Iyer* (CI/integration test owner)
* Release gating approvals: *Connor O’Brien* (Release Engineering)
* Infra dependencies (clusters/storage/mirrors): *Irene Choi* (Infra)
* Rollback correctness triage: *Rafael Mendes* (On-call & reliability)
* Nightly failure triage backup: *Leila Farouk* (QA automation)

Escalation path for a blocking failure:
1) Open incident-style triage thread in #eng-releases and #eng-sre
2) Assign DRI + repo owners; determine if revert is required
3) If within an upgrade window or RC: halt promotion until green or exception approved by SRE + Release Eng

h1. Change process for this matrix
Any change to:
* supported K8s versions
* required RC gating subset
* storage backend coverage
must be made via PR to the pipeline configuration *and* an update to this Confluence page in the same change.

h1. Appendix: Thresholds used in precheck-related tests
These values are test defaults; production thresholds may be configured per customer.

|| Check || Default behavior in tests ||
| Storage headroom | Block if < 15% free on relevant PVs; warn if 15–25% | 
| Node readiness | Block if any CP node NotReady; warn if DP nodes NotReady and DP upgrade is skipped | 
| DB connectivity | Block if cannot connect within 10s or auth fails | 
| Artifact availability | Block if required charts/images not resolvable (online/mirror) or bundle verification fails (air-gapped) | 

h1. Appendix: Known gaps (tracked)
* Deterministic Ceph/Rook environment in CI is still best-effort (work ongoing to reduce flakes).
* GPU-specific DP behaviors are not validated in this suite; this suite validates rollout ordering/readiness gates only.
* Multi-hop upgrades (N-2 → N) are not covered; customers must follow N-1 → N per compatibility policy.

source.title: Burst Pool Sizing Policy (Dedicated Burst Capacity)
source.author: Vanessa Ortiz
source.body:
## Purpose
This document defines Redwood Inference’s fleet policy for sizing and operating regional burst pools that back the **Dedicated Burst Capacity** product. The goals are to (1) enable predictable, paid burst headroom for Dedicated tenants, (2) protect non-burst and baseline Dedicated SLOs through conservative safety margins and hard caps, and (3) provide procurement guidance and triggers as customer demand changes.

This policy applies to **Redwood-managed shared burst pools**, scoped **within a region** and **within a hardware class** (e.g., `us-east/H100`, `eu-west/L40S`). Cross-region bursting is out of scope for the initial release.

## Scope
### In scope
- How to size burst pools by region and hardware class.
- Fleet safety margins and “do no harm” constraints.
- Constraints by region supply risk, deployment topology, and failure domains.
- Capacity procurement implications and lead-time-driven triggers.
- Operational ownership and the ongoing review cadence.

### Out of scope
- Per-customer custom burst pools.
- Any “spot market” or trading model for reservations.
- Arbitrary cross-region capacity borrowing.

## Definitions
- **Dedicated baseline capacity:** GPUs reserved for a specific tenant (contracted committed capacity).
- **Burst pool:** Redwood-managed shared GPU capacity that can be temporarily borrowed by eligible Dedicated tenants.
- **Burst ratio:** Tenant’s maximum burst capacity relative to baseline. Example: baseline 100 GPUs with max burst ratio 0.5 allows up to +50 GPUs from burst pools.
- **Borrowed GPUs:** The instantaneous number of GPUs allocated to burst requests for tenants above their baseline.
- **Pool headroom:** Unallocated GPUs in the burst pool available to satisfy admitted burst requests.
- **Fleet safety cap:** Global guardrail limiting burst allocation to preserve overall platform health and non-burst SLOs.

## Policy summary (what we optimize for)
We size burst pools to meet these priorities, in order:
1. **Protect baseline Dedicated and non-burst tenants.** Burst must be the first thing to degrade under stress.
2. **Keep burst behavior predictable.** Denials should be explainable and stable, not flapping due to small telemetry noise.
3. **Provide commercially useful burst availability.** Target a meaningful probability of burst admission for expected peaks, but not at the expense of (1).
4. **Make procurement incremental and reversible.** Pool growth should be justified by measured demand and forecasted commitments, with clear triggers.

## Ownership and operating cadence
- **Primary owner:** Fleet Capacity (Vanessa Ortiz)
- **Guardrails owner:** Autoscaling & Guardrails (Keiko Yamamoto)
- **Forecasting support:** Capacity Planning (Jamal Pierce)
- **SLO gate / paging policy:** SRE (Sean Gallagher)
- **Review cadence:**
  - Weekly: pool utilization + denial review (Fleet + SRE + Routing)
  - Monthly: sizing model review and procurement trigger evaluation (Fleet + Capacity Planning + Finance)
  - Pre-GA / per-region launch: readiness checklist sign-off (Fleet + SRE + Platform)

## Pool topology and failure domains
Burst pools are provisioned:
- **Per region** (e.g., `us-east`, `us-west`, `eu-west`) and
- **Per hardware class** (e.g., `H100`, `A100`, `L40S`).

Within a region, pools should be segmented across at least **two failure domains** (AZs or equivalent) when the region supports it. In single-AZ regions (or constrained hardware), the pool size policy is more conservative.

## Sizing inputs
Burst pool sizing uses the following inputs per `(region, hw_class)`:
1. **Committed Dedicated baseline (C):** total baseline GPUs reserved for Dedicated tenants in that segment.
2. **Burst entitlement configuration:** distribution of tenant-level max burst ratios and per-tenant caps.
3. **Historical burst demand:** admitted burst GPU-hours, peak borrowed GPUs, and denial reasons.
4. **Utilization and contention:** baseline utilization, queue depth, and neighbor-impact signals (p95/p99 deltas).
5. **Supply risk category:** a region/hardware “supply volatility” factor that influences safety margins.
6. **Procurement constraints:** lead times, minimum order sizes, and stranded capacity risk.

## Default sizing model
### 1) Baseline formula (initial GA default)
For each `(region, hw_class)` segment, set burst pool size **P** as:

**P = max(P_min, ceil(C * r_pool) + P_launch_buffer)**

Where:
- **C** = total Dedicated baseline GPUs in the segment
- **r_pool** = default pool ratio (varies by supply risk and maturity)
- **P_min** = minimum pool size to avoid “always-deny” behavior
- **P_launch_buffer** = small fixed buffer for early GA volatility and measurement error

### 2) Default parameters
Unless explicitly overridden by Fleet Capacity and approved by SRE for a given segment:

| Parameter | Default | Notes |
|---|---:|---|
| r_pool (mature segment) | 0.12 | 12% of committed baseline for established regions/hardware with stable supply and telemetry confidence. |
| r_pool (new segment / constrained supply) | 0.06 | 6% for early rollout segments or high volatility supply. |
| P_min | 8 GPUs | Avoids “noisy” experience for small segments; smaller pools tend to create high denial variance. |
| P_launch_buffer | 4 GPUs | Removed after 2 full weeks of stable GA metrics and no SLO gate trips. |

### 3) Target operating point (not a guarantee)
We aim for the following steady-state characteristics (by segment):
- **Pool utilization (5-minute average):** 40%–70% during normal peak hours
- **Burst admission rate (eligible requests):** 85%+ when the fleet is healthy and under the fleet safety cap
- **Denial mix:** dominated by per-tenant caps/cooldowns, not by pool exhaustion

These are operational targets used to tune capacity and policy; they are not contractual promises.

## Safety margins and hard caps
### Fleet safety cap (global)
Burst pool sizing must assume burst is **preemptible**. We enforce a global cap that limits total burst allocation to a fraction of available fleet headroom.

Fleet sets the global cap per hardware class, but defaults to:
- **Max burst allocation <= 20% of total online GPUs** in that hardware class within a region
- A stricter cap (e.g., **10%**) applies for constrained regions/hardware or during elevated incident risk.

If the global cap is exceeded (or risk signals are triggered), the **global circuit breaker** may reduce or disable bursting automatically per SRE policy.

### Segment safety margin
For each segment, keep a reserved safety margin within the burst pool (not offered to admission) to handle:
- telemetry lag,
- failover events (e.g., node drains),
- short-term baseline traffic spikes.

Default:
- **Offerable pool capacity = P * 0.85**
- **Safety margin = P * 0.15**

In single-AZ segments or high-supply-risk segments:
- **Offerable pool capacity = P * 0.75**
- **Safety margin = P * 0.25**

### Freeze conditions
Fleet may declare a “pool freeze” for a segment (no additional burst enablements, no increases in tenant caps) when any of the following persist for >30 minutes:
- pool utilization > 85% AND pool exhaustion denials increase sharply,
- non-burst p95 latency regression > SLO gate threshold (see SRE doc),
- significant telemetry lag causing unstable admission (see admission controller SLOs).

## Region constraints and supply risk categories
Fleet classifies each `(region, hw_class)` segment into a supply risk category. This affects both `r_pool` and safety margins.

### Supply risk categories
- **Low risk:** stable supply, multiple vendors or established procurement pipeline, multiple AZs.
- **Medium risk:** occasional procurement delays or AZ limitations.
- **High risk:** long lead times, limited AZ support, frequent allocation constraints.

### Default mapping (example; updated quarterly)
| Segment example | Risk | Policy impact |
|---|---|---|
| us-east/H100 | Medium | r_pool = 0.12; safety margin 15% once mature. |
| us-west/H100 | Medium | Start at r_pool = 0.06 during ramp, then increase after 4 weeks stable. |
| eu-west/L40S | High | r_pool = 0.06; safety margin 25%; stricter freeze thresholds. |

Fleet may override classification if procurement realities change (e.g., sudden supply tightening).

## Procurement implications and triggers
### Lead times and planning horizon
Burst pools are capacity that must be available **when demand spikes**, not after. Because GPU procurement and provisioning lead times can be 4–12+ weeks depending on hardware and region, we use a conservative planning horizon.

Default planning assumptions:
- **Provisioning lead time (existing contract capacity):** 2–4 weeks
- **Procurement lead time (new supply / long lead hardware):** 8–12 weeks
- **Regionalization overhead:** 1–2 weeks (moving capacity between regions is not assumed feasible for initial GA)

### Procurement trigger policy
Fleet initiates a procurement request (or capacity reservation adjustment) for a segment when **any** of the following are true for 2 consecutive weeks:
1. **Offerable pool utilization** exceeds 70% during peak windows **and** pool-exhaustion denials exceed 5% of eligible burst requests.
2. Total **admitted burst GPU-hours** grows >25% week-over-week for 3 weeks, and forecast suggests continued growth.
3. Sales/CS flags a near-term contractual need: signed or near-signed Dedicated deals requiring burst, with committed start dates inside the lead time window.

### Stranded capacity avoidance
Burst capacity is shared and preemptible; however, idle burst pools still cost money. To reduce stranded capacity:
- New capacity additions should be incremental (step sizes aligned to node group granularity).
- Prefer expanding segments with demonstrated burst demand and stable admission metrics.
- Avoid adding capacity to segments where denials are primarily policy-based (caps/cooldowns), since adding GPUs will not improve admission rate materially.

### Coordination with Finance
Any procurement trigger that increases monthly run-rate above the approved envelope must be reviewed with Finance (Laura Bennett / Rishi Malhotra). Fleet provides:
- expected utilization curve,
- margin sensitivity under plausible burst demand,
- rollback/repurpose plan if demand does not materialize.

## Operational procedure: quarterly sizing review
### Inputs
- Segment-level dashboard exports (utilization, denials by reason, peak borrowed GPUs, telemetry lag).
- Dedicated baseline committed capacity by segment.
- Forecasted customer growth and burst enablement pipeline (from Product + Sales).
- Incidents / SLO gate events and any circuit breaker activations.

### Steps
1. **Validate data quality:** ensure telemetry lag and cardinality constraints did not hide demand/denials.
2. **Compute current sizing ratio:** P/C and compare against policy defaults.
3. **Assess denial reasons:** confirm whether denials are capacity-driven vs policy-driven.
4. **Adjust segment parameters (if needed):**
   - r_pool, P_min, safety margin, and/or freeze thresholds.
5. **Document change and rollout:** changes require sign-off from Fleet owner and SRE owner; update Terraform variables and annotate dashboards.

### Outputs
- Updated segment sizing table.
- Procurement requests (if triggered).
- Changelog and next review date.

## Operational guardrails that impact sizing outcomes
Sizing and guardrails are coupled. A larger pool does not necessarily improve customer experience if policy is strict; conversely, a permissive policy can cause neighbor impact even with a moderate pool.

Fleet expects the following to remain enforced by Platform/SRE:
- Per-tenant caps (absolute GPU cap and burst ratio cap)
- Cooldown windows to avoid burst oscillation
- Priority tiering that is anti-starvation (no tenant can be denied indefinitely due to tiering)
- Fast rollback and global circuit breaker

## Communication and customer expectation notes (for internal alignment)
- Burst is **best-effort above baseline**. Pool sizing targets are internal; customers should see clear limit status and denial reasons, but not internal pool inventory.
- During fleet stress, burst is the first to be reduced/disabled.
- When Sales requests “guaranteed burst,” Fleet must route the request through Product + Legal for dedicated expansion or contractual commitments; shared burst pools are not intended to provide hard guarantees.

## Exceptions and approvals
Exceptions to this policy (e.g., unusually high r_pool for a segment or a temporary pool increase for a planned event) require:
- Fleet Capacity approval (Vanessa Ortiz)
- SRE approval (Sean Gallagher) if exception increases neighbor-risk
- Finance approval if the change materially increases cost and is not within the pre-approved budget envelope

Document exceptions in the regional rollout plan and include start/end dates and rollback criteria.

## Changelog
- 2025-06-18: Clarified default safety margin ratios and added explicit procurement triggers based on denial mix.
- 2025-05-22: Added supply risk categories and guidance for single-AZ segments.
- 2025-02-03: Initial policy draft aligned to burst GA design constraints (regional/hardware segmentation; preemptible burst).

2. **Keep burst behavior predictable.** Denials should be explainable and stable, not flapping due to small telemetry noise.
3. **Provide commercially useful burst availability.** Target a meaningful probability of burst admission for expected peaks, but not at the expense of (1).
4. **Make procurement incremental and reversible.** Pool growth should be justified by measured demand and forecasted commitments, with clear triggers.

## Ownership and operating cadence
- **Primary owner:** Fleet Capacity (Vanessa Ortiz)
- **Guardrails owner:** Autoscaling & Guardrails (Keiko Yamamoto)
- **Forecasting support:** Capacity Planning (Jamal Pierce)
- **SLO gate / paging policy:** SRE (Sean Gallagher)
- **Review cadence:**
  - Weekly: pool utilization + denial review (Fleet + SRE + Routing)
  - Monthly: sizing model review and procurement trigger evaluation (Fleet + Capacity Planning + Finance)
  - Pre-GA / per-region launch: readiness checklist sign-off (Fleet + SRE + Platform)

## Pool topology and failure domains
Burst pools are provisioned:
- **Per region** (e.g., `us-east`, `us-west`, `eu-west`) and
- **Per hardware class** (e.g., `H100`, `A100`, `L40S`).

Within a region, pools should be segmented across at least **two failure domains** (AZs or equivalent) when the region supports it. In single-AZ regions (or constrained hardware), the pool size policy is more conservative.

## Sizing inputs
Burst pool sizing uses the following inputs per `(region, hw_class)`:
1. **Committed Dedicated baseline (C):** total baseline GPUs reserved for Dedicated tenants in that segment.
2. **Burst entitlement configuration:** distribution of tenant-level max burst ratios and per-tenant caps.
3. **Historical burst demand:** admitted burst GPU-hours, peak borrowed GPUs, and denial reasons.
4. **Utilization and contention:** baseline utilization, queue depth, and neighbor-impact signals (p95/p99 deltas).
5. **Supply risk category:** a region/hardware “supply volatility” factor that influences safety margins.
6. **Procurement constraints:** lead times, minimum order sizes, and stranded capacity risk.

## Default sizing model
### 1) Baseline formula (initial GA default)
For each `(region, hw_class)` segment, set burst pool size **P** as:

**P = max(P_min, ceil(C * r_pool) + P_launch_buffer)**

Where:
- **C** = total Dedicated baseline GPUs in the segment
- **r_pool** = default pool ratio (varies by supply risk and maturity)
- **P_min** = minimum pool size to avoid “always-deny” behavior
- **P_launch_buffer** = small fixed buffer for early GA volatility and measurement error

### 2) Default parameters
Unless explicitly overridden by Fleet Capacity and approved by SRE for a given segment:

| Parameter | Default | Notes |
|---|---:|---|
| r_pool (mature segment) | 0.12 | 12% of committed baseline for established regions/hardware with stable supply and telemetry confidence. |
| r_pool (new segment / constrained supply) | 0.06 | 6% for early rollout segments or high volatility supply. |
| P_min | 8 GPUs | Avoids “noisy” experience for small segments; smaller pools tend to create high denial variance. |
| P_launch_buffer | 4 GPUs | Removed after 2 full weeks of stable GA metrics and no SLO gate trips. |

### 3) Target operating point (not a guarantee)
We aim for the following steady-state characteristics (by segment):
- **Pool utilization (5-minute average):** 40%–70% during normal peak hours
- **Burst admission rate (eligible requests):** 85%+ when the fleet is healthy and under the fleet safety cap
- **Denial mix:** dominated by per-tenant caps/cooldowns, not by pool exhaustion

These are operational targets used to tune capacity and policy; they are not contractual promises.

## Safety margins and hard caps
### Fleet safety cap (global)
Burst pool sizing must assume burst is **preemptible**. We enforce a global cap that limits total burst allocation to a fraction of available fleet headroom.

Fleet sets the global cap per hardware class, but defaults to:
- **Max burst allocation <= 20% of total online GPUs** in that hardware class within a region
- A stricter cap (e.g., **10%**) applies for constrained regions/hardware or during elevated incident risk.

If the global cap is exceeded (or risk signals are triggered), the **global circuit breaker** may reduce or disable bursting automatically per SRE policy.

### Segment safety margin
For each segment, keep a reserved safety margin within the burst pool (not offered to admission) to handle:
- telemetry lag,
- failover events (e.g., node drains),
- short-term baseline traffic spikes.

Default:
- **Offerable pool capacity = P * 0.85**
- **Safety margin = P * 0.15**

In single-AZ segments or high-supply-risk segments:
- **Offerable pool capacity = P * 0.75**
- **Safety margin = P * 0.25**

### Freeze conditions
Fleet may declare a “pool freeze” for a segment (no additional burst enablements, no increases in tenant caps) when any of the following persist for >30 minutes:
- pool utilization > 85% AND pool exhaustion denials increase sharply,
- non-burst p95 latency regression > SLO gate threshold (see SRE doc),
- significant telemetry lag causing unstable admission (see admission controller SLOs).

## Region constraints and supply risk categories
Fleet classifies each `(region, hw_class)` segment into a supply risk category. This affects both `r_pool` and safety margins.

### Supply risk categories
- **Low risk:** stable supply, multiple vendors or established procurement pipeline, multiple AZs.
- **Medium risk:** occasional procurement delays or AZ limitations.
- **High risk:** long lead times, limited AZ support, frequent allocation constraints.

### Default mapping (example; updated quarterly)
| Segment example | Risk | Policy impact |
|---|---|---|
| us-east/H100 | Medium | r_pool = 0.12; safety margin 15% once mature. |
| us-west/H100 | Medium | Start at r_pool = 0.06 during ramp, then increase after 4 weeks stable. |
| eu-west/L40S | High | r_pool = 0.06; safety margin 25%; stricter freeze thresholds. |

Fleet may override classification if procurement realities change (e.g., sudden supply tightening).

## Procurement implications and triggers
### Lead times and planning horizon
Burst pools are capacity that must be available **when demand spikes**, not after. Because GPU procurement and provisioning lead times can be 4–12+ weeks depending on hardware and region, we use a conservative planning horizon.

Default planning assumptions:
- **Provisioning lead time (existing contract capacity):** 2–4 weeks
- **Procurement lead time (new supply / long lead hardware):** 8–12 weeks
- **Regionalization overhead:** 1–2 weeks (moving capacity between regions is not assumed feasible for initial GA)

### Procurement trigger policy
Fleet initiates a procurement request (or capacity reservation adjustment) for a segment when **any** of the following are true for 2 consecutive weeks:
1. **Offerable pool utilization** exceeds 70% during peak windows **and** pool-exhaustion denials exceed 5% of eligible burst requests.
2. Total **admitted burst GPU-hours** grows >25% week-over-week for 3 weeks, and forecast suggests continued growth.
3. Sales/CS flags a near-term contractual need: signed or near-signed Dedicated deals requiring burst, with committed start dates inside the lead time window.

### Stranded capacity avoidance
Burst capacity is shared and preemptible; however, idle burst pools still cost money. To reduce stranded capacity:
- New capacity additions should be incremental (step sizes aligned to node group granularity).
- Prefer expanding segments with demonstrated burst demand and stable admission metrics.
- Avoid adding capacity to segments where denials are primarily policy-based (caps/cooldowns), since adding GPUs will not improve admission rate materially.

### Coordination with Finance
Any procurement trigger that increases monthly run-rate above the approved envelope must be reviewed with Finance (Laura Bennett / Rishi Malhotra). Fleet provides:
- expected utilization curve,
- margin sensitivity under plausible burst demand,
- rollback/repurpose plan if demand does not materialize.

## Operational procedure: quarterly sizing review
### Inputs
- Segment-level dashboard exports (utilization, denials by reason, peak borrowed GPUs, telemetry lag).
- Dedicated baseline committed capacity by segment.
- Forecasted customer growth and burst enablement pipeline (from Product + Sales).
- Incidents / SLO gate events and any circuit breaker activations.

### Steps
1. **Validate data quality:** ensure telemetry lag and cardinality constraints did not hide demand/denials.
2. **Compute current sizing ratio:** P/C and compare against policy defaults.
3. **Assess denial reasons:** confirm whether denials are capacity-driven vs policy-driven.
4. **Adjust segment parameters (if needed):**
   - r_pool, P_min, safety margin, and/or freeze thresholds.
5. **Document change and rollout:** changes require sign-off from Fleet owner and SRE owner; update Terraform variables and annotate dashboards.

### Outputs
- Updated segment sizing table.
- Procurement requests (if triggered).
- Changelog and next review date.

## Operational guardrails that impact sizing outcomes
Sizing and guardrails are coupled. A larger pool does not necessarily improve customer experience if policy is strict; conversely, a permissive policy can cause neighbor impact even with a moderate pool.

Fleet expects the following to remain enforced by Platform/SRE:
- Per-tenant caps (absolute GPU cap and burst ratio cap)
- Cooldown windows to avoid burst oscillation
- Priority tiering that is anti-starvation (no tenant can be denied indefinitely due to tiering)
- Fast rollback and global circuit breaker

## Communication and customer expectation notes (for internal alignment)
- Burst is **best-effort above baseline**. Pool sizing targets are internal; customers should see clear limit status and denial reasons, but not internal pool inventory.
- During fleet stress, burst is the first to be reduced/disabled.
- When Sales requests “guaranteed burst,” Fleet must route the request through Product + Legal for dedicated expansion or contractual commitments; shared burst pools are not intended to provide hard guarantees.

## Exceptions and approvals
Exceptions to this policy (e.g., unusually high r_pool for a segment or a temporary pool increase for a planned event) require:
- Fleet Capacity approval (Vanessa Ortiz)
- SRE approval (Sean Gallagher) if exception increases neighbor-risk
- Finance approval if the change materially increases cost and is not within the pre-approved budget envelope

Document exceptions in the regional rollout plan and include start/end dates and rollback criteria.

## Changelog
- 2025-06-18: Clarified default safety margin ratios and added explicit procurement triggers based on denial mix.
- 2025-05-22: Added supply risk categories and guidance for single-AZ segments.
- 2025-02-03: Initial policy draft aligned to burst GA design constraints (regional/hardware segmentation; preemptible burst).

## Purpose
This document defines Redwood Inference’s fleet policy for sizing and operating regional burst pools that back the **Dedicated Burst Capacity** product. The goals are to (1) enable predictable, paid burst headroom for Dedicated tenants, (2) protect non-burst and baseline Dedicated SLOs through conservative safety margins and hard caps, and (3) provide procurement guidance and triggers as customer demand changes.

This policy applies to **Redwood-managed shared burst pools**, scoped **within a region** and **within a hardware class** (e.g., `us-east/H100`, `eu-west/L40S`). Cross-region bursting is out of scope for the initial release.

## Scope
### In scope
- How to size burst pools by region and hardware class.
- Fleet safety margins and “do no harm” constraints.
- Constraints by region supply risk, deployment topology, and failure domains.
- Capacity procurement implications and lead-time-driven triggers.
- Operational ownership and the ongoing review cadence.

### Out of scope
- Per-customer custom burst pools.
- Any “spot market” or trading model for reservations.
- Arbitrary cross-region capacity borrowing.

## Definitions
- **Dedicated baseline capacity:** GPUs reserved for a specific tenant (contracted committed capacity).
- **Burst pool:** Redwood-managed shared GPU capacity that can be temporarily borrowed by eligible Dedicated tenants.
- **Burst ratio:** Tenant’s maximum burst capacity relative to baseline. Example: baseline 100 GPUs with max burst ratio 0.5 allows up to +50 GPUs from burst pools.
- **Borrowed GPUs:** The instantaneous number of GPUs allocated to burst requests for tenants above their baseline.
- **Pool headroom:** Unallocated GPUs in the burst pool available to satisfy admitted burst requests.
- **Fleet safety cap:** Global guardrail limiting burst allocation to preserve overall platform health and non-burst SLOs.

## Policy summary (what we optimize for)
We size burst pools to meet these priorities, in order:
1. **Protect baseline Dedicated and non-burst tenants.** Burst must be the first thing to degrade under stress.
2. **Keep burst behavior predictable.** Denials should be explainable and stable, not flapping due to small telemetry noise.
3. **Provide commercially useful burst availability.** Target a meaningful probability of burst admission for expected peaks, but not at the expense of (1).
4. **Make procurement incremental and reversible.** Pool growth should be justified by measured demand and forecasted commitments, with clear triggers.

6. **Procurement constraints:** lead times, minimum order sizes, and stranded capacity risk.

## Default sizing model

### 3) Target operating point (not a guarantee)

These are operational targets used to tune capacity and policy; they are not contractual promises.

## Safety margins and hard caps

## Procurement implications and triggers

source.title: Golden IaC Gate and Canary Eval Harness for GPU Kernel Rollouts
source.author: Marcus Patel
source.content:
Overview

This page documents the "Golden IaC Gate" pattern and the Canary Eval Harness used to safely roll kernel-level or runtime upgrades (CUDA, custom kernels, mixed-precision kernels) through our GPU fleet. It is intended for infra engineers owning GPU CI/CD, runtime teams, and oncall responders who will operate or interrupt rollouts.

Goals
- Provide an automated, auditable gate that prevents unsafe Terraform/IaC pushes from reaching production GPU pools.
- Verify runtime/kernel behavior through synthetic microbenchmarks + real-traffic shadowing in a canary pool before broad rollout.
- Minimize blast radius by progressive rollout with metric-based automatic rollback.

Scope
- Applies to changes that affect: kernel binaries, kernel flags (mixed-precision kernels), inference-serving runtime (gpu-serving-runtime images), and low-level GPU node images.
- Does NOT attempt to validate model quality or prompt-level regressions (those are handled by the eval-harness).

Ownership and Roles
- Release owner: infra-release@redwood.com (Engineering release coordinator)
- Primary oncall: gpu-fleet-oncall (eng-sre)
- IaC gate automation: ci-cd-team (ci infra)
- Kernel runtime owners: serving-runtime team

Architecture (high level)
- Source pipeline (git) -> CI job (static checks + unit tests) -> Golden IaC Gate (policy evaluator) -> Buildkite deploy job -> Canary orchestration -> Metrics monitor -> Auto-roll or rollback controller.

Key services and components referenced
- gpu-allocator: responsible for creating canary pools and labeling nodes.
- burst-orchestrator: handles tenant burst migration during a canary.
- tenant-credit-service: enforces credits when moving tenants during canary.
- ci-gatekeeper: Buildkite plugin that runs the IaC Gate policy checks and blocks promotion.
- canary-harness: lightweight traffic proxy that can mirror a subset of production traffic.

Preflight checks (IaC Gate criteria)
- Terraform plan must be created with the "safe-plan" flag and uploaded to the gating bucket.
- All changed modules must have a passing tfsec and unit test report.
- Module contracts: changed module must include a compatibility matrix for versions and a migration script if required.
- Change size limit: node-image-size-change <= 200MB or explicit approval required.

Canary Creation and Traffic Strategy
1) Create dedicated canary pool (default size 3 nodes) in target region tagged: environment=canary, kernel_candidate=<image-tag>.
2) Deploy new kernel/runtime bundle to canary pool and perform a smoke pass (health checks, nvml probes).
3) Mirror 2% of production inference traffic to canary using canary-harness; mirror is non-blocking but should exercise typical request path (batching, KV cache hit/miss).
4) Run synthetic microbench suite (benchmarks below) against canary nodes every 5 minutes for the first hour.
5) Observe metrics window (30-60 minutes) and evaluate acceptance criteria.

Acceptance criteria (all must pass)
- avg_inference_latency_ms (p95) increase <= 5% relative to baseline on matching sequence-length buckets.
- gpu_utilization_pct difference <= 8% (within expected variance band).
- eviction_rate_per_hour <= baseline + 0.05 evictions/hour per node.
- kernel OOM events: zero tolerated.
- nvml_error_count: zero tolerated.
- regressions in token throughput: decrease <= 7% measured at steady state.

SLOs and Key Metrics (example table)
| metric                         | baseline | pass threshold | measurement window | owner             |
|-------------------------------|----------|----------------|--------------------|-------------------|
| p95 inference latency (ms)     | 42       | <= 44          | 60m               | serving-runtime   |
| avg gpu_utilization_pct        | 73       | within +/-8%   | 60m               | gpu-fleet         |
| eviction_rate_per_hour         | 0.02     | <= 0.07        | 60m               | eng-sre           |
| nvml_error_count               | 0        | == 0           | 60m               | infra-telemetry   |

Automated Gate Behavior
- If acceptance criteria are met, the gate issues a promotion token that allows Buildkite deploy jobs to progress to the next stage (staged rollout).
- If any criteria fail, the gate automatically creates a rollback runbook entry, marks the build as failed, and sends alerts to the gpu-fleet-oncall channel with a link to telemetry dashboards and the terraform plan diff.

Terraform/IaC Integration Patterns
- Required: Each GPU node image change must be represented by a "kernel_image" variable in module root and a matching "compatibility_version" file in the module root.
- The IaC Gate reads the plan file and verifies only annotated resources are allowed to change without manual approval (see policy examples).

Example Buildkite pipeline snippet (YAML)
---
steps:
  - label: "Run IaC Gate"
    command: |
      buildkite-agent artifact download tfplan.json . --step "plan"
      ./ci/gatekeeper/run_gate.sh --plan tfplan.json --bucket s3://ci-gates/
    plugins:
      - docker#v3.6.0:
          image: ghcr.io/redwood/ci-gatekeeper:stable

Gate policy pseudocode (shell)
#!/usr/bin/env bash
PLAN=$1
# extract changed resources
changed=$(jq '.resource_changes[] | select(.change.actions | index("update") or index("create") or index("delete"))' $PLAN)
# allow-only list check
if echo "$changed" | grep -q "aws_instance.gpu_node"; then
  # further validation
  ./ci/gatekeeper/validate_image_size.sh || exit 1
fi

Canary orchestration (detailed steps)
1. Allocate canary nodes via gpu-allocator: allocate --count 3 --tag environment=canary --image <candidate>
2. Wait for node readiness: ./scripts/wait_node_ready.sh --timeout 15m
3. Run smoke checks: ssh node ./smoke.sh (checks: cuda visible devices, driver version, nvml probe)
4. Enable mirror: canary-harness mirror enable --percentage 2 --route inference-v2
5. Start benchmark runner: canary-harness bench run --suite micro-kernel --interval 5m --repeat 12
6. Collect metrics and evaluate acceptance script: ./ci/gatekeeper/evaluate_acceptance.py --window 60m --baseline-tag stable

Rollout strategy after passing canary
- Stage 1: 5% of fleet nodes replaced across low-impact pools.
- Stage 2: 25% replacement after 6 hours of stable telemetry.
- Stage 3: 100% replacement with rolling drain and tenant migration over 48 hours.

Rollback procedure (on automated failure)
1. ci-gatekeeper cancels ongoing promotion and posts a rollback issue to the release tracker.
2. gpu-allocator runs a node re-image to previous kernel_image tag: allocate --replace --image <previous-tag>
3. If drain is incomplete, trigger tenant migration via burst-orchestrator with "emergency-migrate" flag.
4. Post-mortem owner assigned and 72-hour hotfix window scheduled.

Manual override and emergency approvals
- For urgent security fixes where kernel change is required immediately, the release owner can approve a bypass using the Release Approvals console (must include a mitigation plan and a list of impacted tenants). Bypass requires two approvers from: infra-leads, serving-runtime-lead, and eng-sre.

CI/CD and Runbook Examples (commands)
- Create plan and upload:
  terraform plan -out=tfplan.binary && terraform show -json tfplan.binary > tfplan.json && buildkite-agent artifact upload tfplan.json
- Force-run gate locally (for debugging):
  ./ci/gatekeeper/run_gate.sh --plan ./tfplan.json --debug --save-artifact ./gate-debug.json
- Emergency rollback command (runbook):
  ./tools/reimage_nodes.sh --selector "tag:environment=canary" --image <previous-tag> --parallel 2

Troubleshooting checklist
- If smoke checks fail with NVML timeout: check driver kernel mismatch, run /usr/local/bin/nvml-diag and open an infra-ticket with nvml logs.
- If mirror traffic causes authentication failures: verify mirroring path preserves auth headers and service account used by canary-harness has tenant-scoped read access.
- If eviction_rate spikes: pause mirror, roll nodes back, and investigate kernel scheduling latencies.

Observability and Dashboards
- Canary dashboard: gpu/canary/<image-tag> showing p50/p95 latency, gpu_utilization, eviction_rate, nvml_errors, swap_usage.
- Promotion audit log: ci/promotion/<build-id> stores plan diff, gate decision, and artifact versions.

Metrics to capture in telemetry (required)
- cpu_steal_time_pct, gpu_scheduled_tasks, process_gpu_mem_mb, nvml_error_count, kernel_page_faults, scheduler_latency_ms.

Change log and release notes template (for changelog entry)
- Title: Kernel runtime upgrade <image-tag>
- Summary: Short description of change, kernels affected, expected performance impact.
- Rollout plan: Canary ID, staging percentages, rollback triggers.
- Owners: Release owner, runtime owner, oncall rotation.

Appendix: Example acceptance evaluation script output (trimmed)
{
  "baseline_p95": 42.1,
  "candidate_p95": 43.8,
  "gpu_util_delta": 4.2,
  "nvml_errors": 0,
  "decision": "pass"
}

Related pages and references
- GPU capacity runbooks and tenancy mapping: /confluence/eng-infra/gpu-fleet-and-capacity/gpu-tenant-eviction-predictor-and-provisioning-guidelines
- IaC gating policies: /confluence/eng-infra/terraform-and-iac/tf-policy-network-canary-gpu-egress-validation

Notes and future work
- Next iteration: integrate scheduler-sim to run synthetic long-tail sequence loads prior to canary creation.
- Add support for multi-node canaries where stateful services must be validated together.

Questions and feedback
- Please send feedback to infra-release@redwood.com or leave inline comments on this page.

title: Golden IaC Gate and Canary Eval Harness for GPU Kernel Rollouts
preview:
Overview

This page documents the "Golden IaC Gate" pattern and the Canary Eval Harness used to safely roll kernel-level or runtime upgrades (CUDA, custom kernels, mixed-precision kernels) through our GPU fleet. It is intended for infra engineers owning GPU CI/CD, runtime teams, and oncall responders who will operate or interrupt rollouts.

Goals
- Provide an automated, auditable gate that prevents unsafe Terraform/IaC pushes from reaching production GPU pools.
- Verify runtime/kernel behavior through synthetic microbenchmarks + real-traffic shadowing in a canary pool before broad rollout.
- Minimize blast radius by progressive rollout with metric-based automatic rollback.

Scope
- Applies to changes that affect: kernel binaries, kernel flags (mixed-precision kernels), inference-serving runtime (gpu-serving-runtime images), and low-level GPU node images.
- Does NOT attempt to validate model quality or prompt-level regressions (those are handled by the eval-harness).

Ownership and Roles
- Release owner: infra-release@redwood.com (Engineering release coordinator)
- Primary oncall: gpu-fleet-oncall (eng-sre)
- IaC gate automation: ci-cd-team (ci infra)
- Kernel runtime owners: serving-runtime team

Architecture (high level)
- Source pipeline (git) -> CI job (static checks + unit tests) -> Golden IaC Gate (policy evaluator) -> Buildkite deploy job -> Canary orchestration -> Metrics monitor -> Auto-roll or rollback controller.

Key services and components referenced
- gpu-allocator: responsible for creating canary pools and labeling nodes.
- burst-orchestrator: handles tenant burst migration during a canary.
- tenant-credit-service: enforces credits when moving tenants during canary.
- ci-gatekeeper: Buildkite plugin that runs the IaC Gate policy checks and blocks promotion.
- canary-harness: lightweight traffic proxy that can mirror a subset of production traffic.

Preflight checks (IaC Gate criteria)
- Terraform plan must be created with the "safe-plan" flag and uploaded to the gating bucket.
- All changed modules must have a passing tfsec and unit test report.
- Module contracts: changed module must include a compatibility matrix for versions and a migration script if required.
- Change size limit: node-image-size-change <= 200MB or explicit approval required.

Canary Creation and Traffic Strategy
1) Create dedicated canary pool (default size 3 nodes) in target region tagged: environment=canary, kernel_candidate=<image-tag>.
2) Deploy new kernel/runtime bundle to canary pool and perform a smoke pass (health checks, nvml probes).
3) Mirror 2% of production inference traffic to canary using canary-harness; mirror is non-blocking but should exercise typical request path (batching, KV cache hit/miss).
4) Run synthetic microbench suite (benchmarks below) against canary nodes every 5 minutes for the first hour.
5) Observe metrics window (30-60 minutes) and evaluate acceptance criteria.

Acceptance criteria (all must pass)
- avg_inference_latency_ms (p95) increase <= 5% relative to baseline on matching sequence-length buckets.
- gpu_utilization_pct difference <= 8% (within expected variance band).
- eviction_rate_per_hour <= baseline + 0.05 evictions/hour per node.
- kernel OOM events: zero tolerated.
- nvml_error_count: zero tolerated.
- regressions in token throughput: decrease <= 7% measured at steady state.

SLOs and Key Metrics (example table)
| metric                         | baseline | pass threshold | measurement window | owner             |
|-------------------------------|----------|----------------|--------------------|-------------------|
| p95 inference latency (ms)     | 42       | <= 44          | 60m               | serving-runtime   |
| avg gpu_utilization_pct        | 73       | within +/-8%   | 60m               | gpu-fleet         |
| eviction_rate_per_hour         | 0.02     | <= 0.07        | 60m               | eng-sre           |
| nvml_error_count               | 0        | == 0           | 60m               | infra-telemetry   |

Automated Gate Behavior
- If acceptance criteria are met, the gate issues a promotion token that allows Buildkite deploy jobs to progress to the next stage (staged rollout).
- If any criteria fail, the gate automatically creates a rollback runbook entry, marks the build as failed, and sends alerts to the gpu-fleet-oncall channel with a link to telemetry dashboards and the terraform plan diff.

Terraform/IaC Integration Patterns
- Required: Each GPU node image change must be represented by a "kernel_image" variable in module root and a matching "compatibility_version" file in the module root.
- The IaC Gate reads the plan file and verifies only annotated resources are allowed to change without manual approval (see policy examples).

Example Buildkite pipeline snippet (YAML)
---
steps:
  - label: "Run IaC Gate"
    command: |
      buildkite-agent artifact download tfplan.json . --step "plan"
      ./ci/gatekeeper/run_gate.sh --plan tfplan.json --bucket s3://ci-gates/
    plugins:
      - docker#v3.6.0:
          image: ghcr.io/redwood/ci-gatekeeper:stable

Gate policy pseudocode (shell)
#!/usr/bin/env bash
PLAN=$1
# extract changed resources
changed=$(jq '.resource_changes[] | select(.change.actions | index("update") or index("create") or index("delete"))' $PLAN)
# allow-only list check
if echo "$changed" | grep -q "aws_instance.gpu_node"; then
  # further validation
  ./ci/gatekeeper/validate_image_size.sh || exit 1
fi

Canary orchestration (detailed steps)
1. Allocate canary nodes via gpu-allocator: allocate --count 3 --tag environment=canary --image <candidate>
2. Wait for node readiness: ./scripts/wait_node_ready.sh --timeout 15m
3. Run smoke checks: ssh node ./smoke.sh (checks: cuda visible devices, driver version, nvml probe)
4. Enable mirror: canary-harness mirror enable --percentage 2 --route inference-v2
5. Start benchmark runner: canary-harness bench run --suite micro-kernel --interval 5m --repeat 12
6. Collect metrics and evaluate acceptance script: ./ci/gatekeeper/evaluate_acceptance.py --window 60m --baseline-tag stable

Rollout strategy after passing canary
- Stage 1: 5% of fleet nodes replaced across low-impact pools.
- Stage 2: 25% replacement after 6 hours of stable telemetry.
- Stage 3: 100% replacement with rolling drain and tenant migration over 48 hours.

Rollback procedure (on automated failure)
1. ci-gatekeeper cancels ongoing promotion and posts a rollback issue to the release tracker.
2. gpu-allocator runs a node re-image to previous kernel_image tag: allocate --replace --image <previous-tag>
3. If drain is incomplete, trigger tenant migration via burst-orchestrator with "emergency-migrate" flag.
4. Post-mortem owner assigned and 72-hour hotfix window scheduled.

Manual override and emergency approvals
- For urgent security fixes where kernel change is required immediately, the release owner can approve a bypass using the Release Approvals console (must include a mitigation plan and a list of impacted tenants). Bypass requires two approvers from: infra-leads, serving-runtime-lead, and eng-sre.

CI/CD and Runbook Examples (commands)
- Create plan and upload:
  terraform plan -out=tfplan.binary && terraform show -json tfplan.binary > tfplan.json && buildkite-agent artifact upload tfplan.json
- Force-run gate locally (for debugging):
  ./ci/gatekeeper/run_gate.sh --plan ./tfplan.json --debug --save-artifact ./gate-debug.json
- Emergency rollback command (runbook):
  ./tools/reimage_nodes.sh --selector "tag:environment=canary" --image <previous-tag> --parallel 2

Troubleshooting checklist
- If smoke checks fail with NVML timeout: check driver kernel mismatch, run /usr/local/bin/nvml-diag and open an infra-ticket with nvml logs.
- If mirror traffic causes authentication failures: verify mirroring path preserves auth headers and service account used by canary-harness has tenant-scoped read access.
- If eviction_rate spikes: pause mirror, roll nodes back, and investigate kernel scheduling latencies.

Observability and Dashboards
- Canary dashboard: gpu/canary/<image-tag> showing p50/p95 latency, gpu_utilization, eviction_rate, nvml_errors, swap_usage.
- Promotion audit log: ci/promotion/<build-id> stores plan diff, gate decision, and artifact versions.

Metrics to capture in telemetry (required)
- cpu_steal_time_pct, gpu_scheduled_tasks, process_gpu_mem_mb, nvml_error_count, kernel_page_faults, scheduler_latency_ms.

Change log and release notes template (for changelog entry)
- Title: Kernel runtime upgrade <image-tag>
- Summary: Short description of change, kernels affected, expected performance impact.
- Rollout plan: Canary ID, staging percentages, rollback triggers.
- Owners: Release owner, runtime owner, oncall rotation.

Appendix: Example acceptance evaluation script output (trimmed)
{
  "baseline_p95": 42.1,
  "candidate_p95": 43.8,
  "gpu_util_delta": 4.2,
  "nvml_errors": 0,
  "decision": "pass"
}

Related pages and references
- GPU capacity runbooks and tenancy mapping: /confluence/eng-infra/gpu-fleet-and-capacity/gpu-tenant-eviction-predictor-and-provisioning-guidelines
- IaC gating policies: /confluence/eng-infra/terraform-and-iac/tf-policy-network-canary-gpu-egress-validation

Notes and future work
- Next iteration: integrate scheduler-sim to run synthetic long-tail sequence loads prior to canary creation.
- Add support for multi-node canaries where stateful services must be validated together.

Questions and feedback
- Please send feedback to infra-release@redwood.com or leave inline comments on this page.
